reasoning:
The base rate estimate from the historical trend suggests a 35% chance of exceeding the current high value, but this must be strongly adjusted for specific impending liquidity flows. The April 15 tax deadline reliably forces massive amounts of capital out of the banking sector and into the Treasury General Account (TGA), historically dropping bank reserves by $300B–$450B in mid-April. This makes the April 20 and May 13 resolution dates highly likely to be firmly below the pre-tax peak (probabilities of 0.12 and 0.26, respectively). 

As we move into summer, the mechanics flip due to the debt ceiling. Because the debt limit was reinstated in January 2025, the Treasury is forced to rely on "extraordinary measures" and drain the TGA to continue funding the government. As the TGA is drawn down toward zero heading into the estimated "X-Date" in late summer, those funds inject directly back into bank reserves. This creates a high likelihood that by July 12, reserve balances will spike to temporary new highs, offsetting the background drag of Quantitative Tightening (QT), pushing the probability up to slightly above even (0.52). 

By the final October 10 date, the debt ceiling conflict is overwhelmingly expected to have been resolved. Following resolution, the Treasury will issue a deluge of T-bills to rapidly replenish the TGA back to its ~$700 billion target. With the ON RRP facility already mostly drained, this liquidity extraction will hit bank reserves directly, plunging them back downward. Despite this structurally bearish outlook for October reserves, the long 6-month forecast horizon introduces significant macroeconomic unpredictability (e.g. premature end to QT or broader monetary easing), anchoring the final estimate closer to maximum uncertainty (0.44).
